claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 5 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 10 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 10 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK

Child is not in local authority care

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To start, I need to understand your situation. 

**Do you have a child (or children) you're claiming for?** If yes, could you tell me:

1. **The name(s) of your child/children** (so I can keep track if you have more than one)
2. **How old they are?**

This will help me determine which age category they fall into:
- Children under 16 generally qualify automatically (with some conditions)
- Young people aged 16-19 may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Those 20 or over typically don't qualify

What are the ages of the child or children you're asking about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, yes, I'm inquiring about my two children. I have Alex, who's 5 years old, and Blake, who's 10. They both live at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have:
- **Alex, age 5**
- **Blake, age 10**

Both children are under 16, which is great - they fall into the automatic qualification age category. Now I need to ask a few more questions to complete the assessment.

Let me check the next requirement for children under 16:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now, for children under 16, I need to check a few conditions:

**Are Alex and Blake working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

Given their ages (5 and 10), I assume they're not in paid work, but I need to confirm this to proceed correctly through the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they're not working at all - they're just kids! Alex is only 5 and Blake is 10, so neither of them has any kind of job.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Of course! That makes perfect sense. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Next question:

**Are Alex and Blake NOT receiving any benefits in their own right?** 

This would be benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit that would be claimed in the child's own name. Again, given their young ages (5 and 10), this is unlikely, but I need to confirm they're not receiving any such benefits.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them receives any benefits in their own name. They're too young for anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Are either Alex or Blake in any kind of apprenticeship?**

Given their ages (5 and 10), they're definitely too young for apprenticeships, but I need to confirm this to move forward in the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, of course not - they're far too young for apprenticeships! Alex is only 5 and Blake is 10, so neither of them is in any kind of training program like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely right! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check about **your residency status** (as the claimant). 

**Do you normally live in the UK?**

If you meet ANY of the following situations, you'll satisfy the residency requirement:
- You normally live in the UK
- You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
- You have pre-settled status and meet certain conditions (working, studying, sufficient resources, etc.)
- You're a Crown servant living abroad

Could you tell me about your residency situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, no - I don't live in the UK. I'm currently living abroad.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Since you're living abroad, I need to check if you meet any of the special circumstances that would still allow you to claim Child Benefit.

**Could you tell me more about your situation abroad?**

Specifically:
- **Are you a Crown servant** (working for the UK government abroad, such as in the diplomatic service or armed forces)?
- **Do you live in certain qualifying countries** where UK Child Benefit can still be claimed?

Or do any of these apply:
- Do you have **settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme?
- Do you have **pre-settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme (and are you working, studying, or have sufficient resources)?

What is your current situation while living abroad?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, none of those apply to me. I'm not a Crown servant, I don't have settled or pre-settled status, and I'm just living abroad as a regular person. I'm not working for the UK government or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing that information. Unfortunately, I need to give you the outcome based on what you've told me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Assessment Result: **NOT ELIGIBLE**

I'm sorry to inform you that **you are not eligible for Child Benefit** for either Alex or Blake at this time.

### Why You're Not Eligible:

**The issue is with the residency requirements, not your children.** Both Alex (age 5) and Blake (age 10) meet all the child-related criteria:
- ✓ They're under 16
- ✓ They're not working
- ✓ They're not receiving benefits in their own right
- ✓ They're not in apprenticeships

**However**, you do not meet the UK residency requirements because:
- You're living abroad
- You're not a Crown servant working for the UK government
- You don't have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
- You're not in any of the other special circumstance categories

### What This Means:

To claim UK Child Benefit, you must normally live in the UK or meet one of the special circumstances such as:
- Being a Crown servant living abroad
- Having settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
- Having pre-settled status and meeting additional criteria (working, studying with resources, etc.)
- Living in certain qualifying countries

### What You Can Do:

- If your circumstances change and you return to live in the UK, you would then meet the residency requirement and could claim Child Benefit
- Check if the country where you're currently living has any family benefit schemes you might be eligible for

If you have any questions or if your situation changes, please feel free to reach out again.

reasonings: Alex is 5 years old, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship - meeting all child-related criteria. However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements as they live abroad and are not a Crown servant, do not have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, and do not live in qualifying countries. Therefore, the claim for Alex is ineligible due to the claimant's residency status.

Blake is 10 years old, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship - meeting all child-related criteria. However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements as they live abroad and are not a Crown servant, do not have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, and do not live in qualifying countries. Therefore, the claim for Blake is ineligible due to the claimant's residency status.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children, Alex (age 5) and Blake (age 10), who both meet all the child-related eligibility criteria for Child Benefit. However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements, as they live abroad without being a Crown servant, having settled/pre-settled status, or living in qualifying countries. UK Child Benefit requires the claimant to normally live in the UK or meet special residency circumstances. Therefore, the claim is ineligible for both children due to the claimant's failure to meet the residency requirements.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.reason: Does not meet UK residency requirements
response.guidance: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances (settled status, pre-settled status with qualifying criteria, Crown servant, or living in certain countries). If you have pre-settled status, you may need to be working, studying, or have sufficient resources.
